title: Fukaya categories and variation of symplectic form
speaker: Chris Woodward
abstract: I hope to talk more about how to find generators for Fukaya categories using symplectic version of the minimal model program in examples such as symplectic quotients of products of spheres and moduli spaces of parabolic bundles.

proof_stages.label: Morse-theoretic cohomology basis model
proof_stages.summary: The talk begins with the familiar problem of finding a cohomology basis and recalls that a Morse complex can compute cohomology, with unstable manifolds giving explicit cycles in favorable cases.

proof_stages.label: MMP cohomology decomposition by surgeries
proof_stages.summary: The speaker introduces symplectic variation in the anticanonical direction, explains blowdowns and flips with centers, and uses the resulting MMP to obtain a different cohomology basis via exceptional summands.

proof_stages.label: Practical production of MMP runnings
proof_stages.summary: To show that MMP input can be found concretely, the speaker explains that equivariant anticanonical variation upstairs descends through symplectic or GIT quotients and that singular quotient times identify the surgeries.

proof_stages.label: Fukaya-category setup and generation problem
proof_stages.summary: The talk switches to compact rational symplectic manifolds and rational Lagrangian branes, defines Fukaya algebras, weak Maurer-Cartan solutions, and Fukaya categories, and formulates the problem of finding split-generators.

proof_stages.label: Categorical reverse-flip/blow-up theorem
proof_stages.summary: For x_+ obtained from x_- by a reverse flip or blow-up with point center, the speaker states that exceptional Lagrangian torus bundles carry finitely many unobstructed brane structures and, together with canonical variations of generators of Fuk(x_-), split-generate Fuk(x_+).

proof_stages.label: MMP-running corollary and semisimple picture
proof_stages.summary: The theorem is applied to simple toric blow-ups and is proposed to imply that an MMP running with point centers decomposes the Fukaya category into torus-type summands, making its Hochschild cohomology semisimple.

proof_stages.label: Generation via open-closed and quantum splitting
proof_stages.summary: The proof of split-generation is reduced to Abouzaid's generation criterion: the open-closed map from the Hochschild homology of old and exceptional branes hits the x_- quantum-cohomology part and the exceptional part up to a controllably small error.

proof_stages.label: Weak unobstructedness via SFT degeneration
proof_stages.summary: The remaining hard step is sketched by stretching along an S^1-fibered coisotropic, replacing the Fukaya algebra by a broken SFT model whose trivial weak Maurer-Cartan solution transfers back through an A-infinity homotopy equivalence.

examples.name: Toric double blow-up of S^2 x S^2
examples.note: The toric surface with hexagonal moment polytope is used to make concrete both the Morse-theoretic fixed-point basis and the MMP exceptional-divisor basis for cohomology.
examples.engagement_reason: the speaker analyzes the toric double blow-up via its moment hexagon, counts six fixed points/critical points, identifies Morse cycles, and runs the MMP blowdowns on the polytope

examples.name: Pentagon space as (S^2)^5 // SO(3)
examples.note: The symplectic quotient of five spheres, interpreted as a moduli space of closed pentagons, illustrates how generic variation of side lengths produces MMP surgeries in a non-toric-looking case.
examples.engagement_reason: the speaker works through the pentagon quotient by varying side lengths, identifying singular collinear configurations and the resulting MMP surgeries down to S^2 x S^2/P^2 blown up at four points

examples.name: Toric flip picture
examples.note: In response to a question, a toric birational transformation replacing a higher-rank projective bundle by a lower-rank one is sketched to clarify the meaning of a flip versus a reverse flip.
examples.engagement_reason: the speaker draws and reasons through a toric flip picture, describing blowing up a P^2 to a P^1-bundle and then blowing down in the P^2 direction to leave a P^1

examples.name: Generators for a blow-up of S^2 x S^2
examples.note: The known product-equator generators for S^2 x S^2 and their extension to a blow-up illustrate how the theorem adds one exceptional Lagrangian brane when one exceptional cohomology class is added.
examples.engagement_reason: the speaker applies the generation theorem to a blow-up of S^2 x S^2, tracking the product equator generators and the added exceptional Lagrangian to get five generators
